text:
Powered byOutside

The jersey winners at the 2024 Tour de France, namely Remco Evenepoel (Soudal Quick-Step), Richard Carapaz (EF Education-EasyPost), Biniam Girmay (Intermarché-Wanty) and Tadej Pogacar (UAE Team Emirates). Photo by Chris Auld.

There was a lot that was familiar about the finish of the Tour de France on Sunday. Veteran riders completed the final stage of their final Tour,Romain BardetandMark Cavendishamong them. Rookies celebrated finishing their first Tour, includingRemco EvenepoelandDerek Gee. The podium presentations and winner’s national anthem were emotional.



There was the popping of Champagne in the team enclosures, even those that didn’t win anything. Family and friends came to visit their loved ones. What was different was the backdrop of fountains in the Espace Masséna of Nice, not the Arc de Triomphe on the Champs-Élysées.

So, though the Tour de France did not finish in Paris for the first time in its history (and Nice was a great stand-in), the French capital still loomed large over the finale on the French Riviera. That’s because in the coming days many of the riders will be in Paris for theOlympic Games, which starts this coming weekend.

I will write later about the Tour-seasoned contenders for the 2024 Olympic time trial and road race, but at this historic juncture I first want to chronicle the different finishes in Paris that defined the Tour in its first 110 editions.

For the Tour’s first five years, the end of the final stage was in the southwest Paris suburb of Ville d’Avray, from where the finishers (as few as 14 in 1906) rode a few kilometers to the Parc des Princes velodrome to be honored by a packed house. One year, the fans were entertained by the finishers competing in a 1-kilometer time trial, which was cut to a one-lap TT the following year.

From 1908, all the final stage concluders were in the velodrome itself. One of the strangest finishes came in 1909, when the third-place rider overall, Henri Alavoine, crashed 10 kilometers from the Parc des Princes and walked that final stretch with his broken bike over his shoulder. (Back then, a rider had to finish every stage on the bike he started on and had to make all of his own repairs.)

The Parc des Princes was the finish location 54 times, ending in 1967, when the velodrome was demolished to make way for a custom soccer stadium. The next seven editions ended at the much smaller Piste Municipale velodrome on the eastern side of Paris in the Bois de Vincennes. That’s where the culminating 1968 time trial won by Jan Janssen saw the Dutchman displace Belgian Herman Van Springel from the yellow jersey, while all five of Eddy Merckx’ winning Tours ended in Vincennes.

It was only in 1975 that the Avenue des Champs-Élysées first became the most prestigious finish line in cycling. The most dramatic of the 49 finishes there came in 1989 when Greg LeMond overcame a 50-second deficit on GC to beat yellow jersey Laurent Fignon by 58 seconds in the closing 24.5 kilometer time trial to win the Tour by the smallest-ever winning margin: eight seconds. The Tour will return there in 2025 for the 50th finale on the Champs-Élysées.

From that finish line, you can cross the street and stroll down the short Avenue Winston Churchill to the Seine River and cross the Pont Alexandre III—where the Olympic Games time trial will start and finish this Saturday. And if you have a few minutes to spare, you can walk a block and a half to the Avenue Montaigne, where 163 years ago under the gaslights on a dark late-winter evening the very first pedal strokes were made on the original vélocipède [from which we get the name of our publication!].

The field of 35 riders will start at 90-second intervals in the Olympic time trial, which heads east on a flat 32.4-kilometer course that favors the specialist time trialists. The route stays on the Left Bank, past Notre Dame Cathedral, crosses the Seine to the Place de la Bastille and then starts a counterclockwise loop.

This is mostly in the grounds of the Bois de Vincennes—where it passes the Piste Municipale (now named for the original five-time Tour champion Jacques Anquetil), which besides hosting seven Tour finishes was the track used for the 1924 Paris Olympics, the last time the Summer Games were held in France, 100 years ago. The finale after the Bastille returns on the same streets to the Pont Alexandre III.

The last Olympic TT gold medalist Primož Roglič is not defending his title: the one slot for Slovenia goes to Jans Tratnik, who placed 24th in last Sunday’s Tour time trial, but will do much better on a flat course. Among the favorites who also rode the Tour are the Belgian pair, Remco Evenepoel andWout van Aert, Denmark’s Mattias Skjelmose, Switzerland’s Stefan Küng, Germany’s Nils Politt and Canada’s Derek Gee.

However, the men who are even more likely to medal are those who have been preparing separately for this TT: Italy’s Filippo Ganna, Great Britain’s Josh Tarling, Norway’s Tobias Foss and U.S. national championBrandon McNulty.

Two days after the TT comes the mountain bike cross-country, where Britain’sTom Pidcock(who pulled out of the Tour with COVID) is favored to keep the Olympic gold medal he won in Japan three years ago.

The most prestigious cycling event in Paris is the men’s road race, which uses as a course that will completely close down the center of the city on a spectacular course on Saturday, August 3.

Even more than the TT, this 275-kilometer course encompasses an even greater range of cycling sites. From the start at the north end of the Pont d’Iéna, it crosses the Seine before turning left at the foot of the Eiffel Tower (this corner will be the race’s final right turn onto a 230-meter straightaway across the bridge to the finish line). After a neutralized section, the race heads west past the Tour de France organizer ASO offices in Issy-les-Moulineaux before tackling the first short climb, the Côte des Gardes, to the Palace of Versailles.

The route then makes a wide double loop to the west and southwest, closing with four climbs around the Chevreuse Valley, including the Côte de Châteaufort, which was a key challenge in the once great French classics, Bordeaux–Paris and the Grand Prix des Nations time trial, both of which were won by Anquetil, who has a memorial on that hill’s summit.

On returning to the city center and crossing the Seine past the Louvre Museum pyramid, the riders face a 50-kilometer finale that heads into Montmartre. They’ll pass a restaurant once named the Café de Madrid, where the concept for the first Tour de France was conceived at a meeting of sports journalists from the nearby office of L’Auto newspaper (now named L’Équipe) in 1902.

The Olympic road race’spièce de résistanceis two laps of an 18.4-kilometer circuit that climbs a cobbled hill (1 kilometer long at an average 6.5-percent grade) to the basilica of Sacré Coeur. This will be scaled a third time before the race makes a 9.5-kilometer return on city streets back past the Louvre, across the Seine and along the left bank to the finish across the Pont d’Iéna to the Trocadéro Gardens. There, the winner will raise his arms against the backdrop of the Eiffel Tower.

Given the race’s six-plus-hour duration, Tour de France riders (and those who do well in the similarly long monumental classics) are favored. But because of a small field of 90 men, with only four riders in the largest teams, and no race radios, a very open, aggressive race is expected.

Defending champion Richard Carapaz is not competing because Ecuador had only one slot and that’s taken by its national champion, Jhonatan Narvaez, who out-sprintedTadej Pogačarto win the opening stage of this year’s Giro d’Italia. Pogačar has decided not to ride the Olympics so that he can focus on completing the Giro-Tour-worlds triple crown with a rainbow jersey in Zürich on a much more difficult course on September 29. Also missing will be Jonas Vingegaard, second in the Tour, who won’t be part of the Danish team.

Without Pogačar, the most favored Tour de France riders are Evenepoel and Wout van Aert of Belgium. Mathieu van der Poel of the Netherlands, Tom Pidcock of Great Britain, Mads Pedersen of Denmark (who pulled out of the Tour after week 1 following a heavy crash on stage 5),Matteo Jorgensonof the United States, Michal Kwiatkowski (the sole rider for Poland), Biniam Girmay of Eritrea (also without teammates), Ben Healy of Ireland, Michael Matthews of Australia, Matej Mohorič of Slovenia and Alberto Bettiol of Italy will also be there.

Of those who didn’t ride the Tour, the strongest medal contenders are Narváez of Ecuador, Julian Alaphilippe of France and Laurence Pithie of New Zealand.
title: Tales of the Unexpected: Paris on the Horizon
author: John Wilcockson
description: The top Tour de France finishers, except Pogačar and Vingegaard, are heading to Paris for the Olympic Games time trial and road race.
